judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports hit all three expectations with equivalent accuracy and correct calibration; they are comparable, with Report 2 slightly more thorough in confirmation detail.

report_1.evidence: Cross-tenant data leak: the search query is not tenant-scoped.** File: `backend/app/appointments.py`, `search_appointments` (branch lines 10–21). The function receives `tenant_id` but the raw SQL it executes is ... there is no `tenant_id` condition anywhere

report_2.evidence:
Cross-tenant data exposure (PHI leak) — the new search path has no tenant filter.**
   Location: `backend/app/appointments.py`, `search_appointments` (lines 10–20 on the branch) ... there is no `tenant_id` predicate, so it scans the whole `appointments` table across all tenants

report_1.evidence: `backend/app/exports.py` line 11 (`nightly_export` still calls `build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id)`). Concrete failure: every invocation of `nightly_export` raises `TypeError: build_appointment_query() missing 1 required positional argument: 'filters'`

report_2.evidence:
versus `backend/app/exports.py:9`, where `nightly_export` still calls `build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id)` with two arguments.
   Failure: once merged, every invocation of `nightly_export` raises `TypeError: build_appointment_query() missing 1 required positional argument: 'filters'`

report_1.evidence: Today this is not exploitable through the HTTP endpoint, and I confirm that precisely: `backend/app/schemas.py` defines `AppointmentStatus` as a `str` Enum with only `booked`/`cancelled`/`completed`, and FastAPI rejects any other value ... the safe pattern (`:q` bind parameter) is used one line below

report_2.evidence: this is **not exploitable over HTTP today** — `backend/app/api.py` types the parameter as `status: AppointmentStatus`, and `backend/app/schemas.py` defines that enum with exactly three fixed values (`booked`/`cancelled`/`completed`) ... while the adjacent `q` value is correctly bound as `:q`
